items.q: What is the main criticism of Rostow's stages of economic growth?
items.correct: It assumes all countries can follow the same linear Western industrialisation path, ignoring colonial legacies, global trade inequalities and environmental constraints — a top-down modernisation view.
items.misconception: All LICs will pass through Rostow's five stages if they wait long enough.
items.random_wrong: Rostow's model is universally accepted as the best development theory.
items.why: Rostow assumed Western industrialisation as a template, but global economic structures may prevent LICs replicating it. Dependency theorists argue underdevelopment is actively produced — not just a delay.

items.q: What happens at a constructive (divergent) plate boundary?
items.correct: Plates move apart, magma rises to fill the gap and solidifies, forming new oceanic crust — for example at the Mid-Atlantic Ridge.
items.misconception: At constructive boundaries the plates collide and build mountains.
items.random_wrong: At constructive boundaries the plates slide past each other sideways.
items.why: Students confuse 'constructive' with 'collision'. Constructive means new crust is constructed; the plates move apart, not together. Sliding sideways is a conservative/transform boundary.

items.q: What happens during an El Nino event?
items.correct: Trade winds weaken, warm water that normally pools in the western Pacific spreads east, disrupting weather patterns globally — drought in Australia, floods in South America.
items.misconception: El Nino is when the ocean gets colder near Peru.
items.random_wrong: El Nino is the name of a specific hurricane that hit Mexico.
items.why: El Nino brings WARM water to Peru — the cold-water state is La Nina. Students mix up the two phases; the name 'the Christ child' refers to warm currents at Christmas.

items.q: What does the abbreviation BMI stand for?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Body Mass Index. “Basal Metabolic Indicator” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Low height-for-age. “Low weight-for-height” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Approximate global number of overweight adults (in billions)?
items.why: Exact answer: 1.9. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
